observations.recommendation:
1.9 We recommended and the City Accountant agreed to record the necessary
entries in the General Fund books as follows:

1.9.1. Transfer the cost of the 14 completed projects from the CIP accounts
to the corresponding PPE accounts; and

1.9.2. Recognize depreciation expenses totaling ₱794,439.

1.10 We also recommended and the City Accountant agreed, that henceforth,
regular monitoring of the City’s CIP accounts will be made to ensure the
timely transfer of the completed projects to the corresponding PPE accounts.

2.8 We recommended that the CGSO fast-track the submission of the RSI and SSMI to
the CAO for the proper recognition of project expenses. We further recommended
that the City Accountant closely coordinate with CGSO and CPDO to expedite the
reconciliation and preparation of the required entries to arrive at the correct
balances and status of the 64 completed projects under the CIP accounts.

2.9 Also, we recommended that these completed projects be transferred to the
corresponding PPE accounts and consequently recognize their depreciation
expenses reckoned from the project’s date of completion.

3.9 We recommended that the City Accountant record the necessary entries in the
Trust Fund and General Fund books as follows:

3.9.1. Trust Fund Books:
3.9.1.1. Record the transfer of the cost of the three completed projects
from the CIP – Infrastructure Assets account to the Road
Networks account
3.9.1.2. Record the transfer of the Road Networks account to the GF.
3.9.2. General Fund Books:
3.9.2.1. Record the receipt of the three completed projects from TF.
3.9.2.2. Record the corresponding depreciation expenses.
3.10 Henceforth, we recommended that the City Accountant regularly monitor the
City’s CIP accounts to ensure the timely transfer of completed projects to the
corresponding PPE accounts.

4.10 We recommended that the City Treasurer, supported by the Sangguniang
Panlungsod and the City Legal Officer, (i) develop an effective tax collection
campaign program to enhance the collection of RPT/SET Receivables; (ii) post
the list of all real property tax delinquencies at the City Hall and in a publicly
accessible and conspicuous place in each barangay and publish the notice of
delinquency in a newspaper of general circulation once a week for two (2)
consecutive weeks; and (iii) enforce remedies in the collection of real property
through the issuance of a warrant on the real property subject to tax on or
before, or simultaneously with, the institution of the civil action for the
collection of the delinquent tax.

5.19. We recommended that:

5.19.1. Management conduct an investigation on the occurrence of loss of AFs
and institute sanctions, as warranted;
5.19.2. The City Treasurer account for the 11,310 receipts, ₱94,854.00 worth
of ₱2.00-denominated cash tickets, and ₱395,595.00 worth of ₱5.00-
denominated cash tickets; otherwise, restitute the corresponding
money values immediately;
5.19.3. The City Treasurer evaluate and strengthen/formulate internal control
procedures specifically on the receipt, recording, and issuance of AFs,
guided by the handbook on ICSPPS to minimize the risk on inaccurate
and unreliable financial data;
5.19.4. The City Treasurer observe the proper recording of all AFs received
and issued, duly supported with the IFAR, and revise/adjust the
monthly CRAAF to reflect the accurate balances of all AFs in its
custody; and
5.19.5. The City Accountant verify and monitor the completeness of all AFs
issued as reported in the Report of Collections and Deposits.

We recommended that the City Treasurer, with the assistance of the LO, thoroughly review at all times the remittances made by the COs by checking the collections against the accountable forms in their custody, verify the accuracy of their daily and monthly reports, and require them to remit their collections daily. We likewise recommended that the City Treasurer require all COs to turn over their collections to the LO and for the latter to deposit the consolidated collections intact and daily to its AGDB. Henceforth, we recommended that the City Treasurer constantly monitor the strict compliance by the LO and the COs with the provisions of laws, rules, and regulations in collecting and depositing government funds to avoid loss and/or misappropriation thereof.

We recommended that Management strictly implement the required minimum NTHP of Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00) mandated under Section 55 of the General Provisions of R.A. No. 11639. Likewise, we recommended that Management direct the concerned officials involved in the processing of loans to ensure that this provision of law is invariably enforced and to register their disapproval of new loan applications/other payroll deductions, as warranted under the circumstances.

We recommended that Management, through the LDRRMO, include the cash donations as a fund source in the preparation of the LDRRMFIP, unless a specific purpose has been indicated by the donor, direct the Budget and Accounting Offices to maintain the necessary financial records and prepare the necessary monthly reports stipulated under COA Circular No. 2014-002, and instruct the City Accountant to prepare a correcting entry to reclassify the cash donations received as part of the LDRRMF.

We recommended that: 29) Direct the City Accountant to make the adjusting entry to derecognize the in-kind donations and instead disclose adequate information thereon in the Notes to the FS; and 30) Require the CGSO, in coordination with the LDRRMO and CSWDO, to maintain the RDRGD and prepare the monthly the RRDIDRD duly supported with the acknowledgment receipts and IFIDDs for transparency and accountability and as an input to the disclosure in the Notes to FS.

We recommended that the LDRRMO follow-up its request with OCD for the scheduled training at the soonest possible time so that it would be well-equipped in the preparation and submission of the City’s PSCP pursuant to NDRRMC Memorandum No. 33, s. 2018. We further recommended that the City Mayor institutionalize the PSCP by creating a working group that will perform the following activities: a. Conduct a risk assessment; b. Identify critical processes and functions; c. Determine scenarios that may disrupt normal operation; d. Conduct risk analysis and impact analysis; e. Formulate the PSCP based on the identified risks on critical processes and functions and the related steps to be followed to eliminate, if not mitigate, the impact of the determined disruptions following the prescribed templates and requirements in the PSCP guidebook; and f. Submit the plan to the CDRRMO for review, to the LCE for approval, and to the Sangguniang Panlungsod to pass a resolution to institutionalize the PSCP.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) comply with the mandatory posting of the updated APP, NOA, and other information relative to the Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act in the GPPB Online Portal as required in GPPB Circular No. 1-2020. Henceforth, we recommended that the BAC should always be updated with the issuances and advisories of the GPPB by visiting their website regularly to avoid any more non-compliance in the future.

We recommended that Management establish a procedure for identifying PHWs and PSWs who shall be exposed to high risk or low risk hazard including the duration of such exposure duly approved by the Secretary of Health to support claims for hazard pay. We further recommended that the City Accountant require all PHWs and PSWs to support their claims for hazard pay with proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks with certifications from appropriate agencies and the documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 as an indispensable requirement.

We recommended that, henceforth, Management ensure that efficient and systematic procedures are in place while strictly adhering to existing guidelines set forth under DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 09 series of 2020 in the implementation of SAP and similar future undertakings of the government.  As for the subject transaction, we recommended that Management comply with the documentary requirements pursuant to the audit suspensions that may be issued.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management gather complete documentation, especially the PARs/MRs indicating the value of the properties requested for relief and the Head of Agency’s comments and recommendations on the individual requests filed, and resubmit the Request for Relief from Accountability on the damaged/washed out items with justification on the delay in the submission of both the Notice of Loss and the Request for Relief, pursuant to COA Memorandum No. 92-751.

We recommended that Management require:
The Inventory Committee and the CGSO to prioritize the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the accounting records and pinpoint persons responsible for the missing properties who should be made to pay the value thereof as shown in the accounting records;
The CGSO to coordinate with the Accounting Office to fully account for the CY 2018-2021 PPE purchases, complete with data on the property tag, ARE, accountable officer, and office/location of the equipment, otherwise, those that cannot be accounted for shall be a personal liability of the person accountable for said property; and
The Accounting Office to prepare promptly the journal entries to take up the SSMI, IIRUP and other asset disposals submitted by the CGSO with complete documentation that would warrant the dropping of the same from the books.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to compute the depreciation allowance of all its undepreciated Infrastructure Asset accounts for calendar year 2021 and backwards. Prior years’ depreciation should be taken up as Prior Period Adjustment in the books to be closed to Government Equity at the end of the reporting period.

We recommended that Management direct the City Accounting, Engineering and General Services Offices to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on local roads asset management system, and to earnestly perform their respective duties and responsibilities set forth under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct the annual physical count of all its Local Road Networks in accordance with regulations, making sure to produce the details necessary to be reported separately in the RPCLRN for submission to the Auditor and Accounting Office not later than January 31 of each year. The RPCLRN shall be reconciled with the accounting records and the necessary adjusting entries be prepared before the statement date.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the City Accountant to record the transfer of the various PPE accounts under the Trust Fund to the General Fund. Corresponding depreciation expenses shall then be provided in the books.  We further recommended that the details of the beginning balances of Other Structures totaling ₱2,765,803.85 be provided before these are transferred to the General Fund.

We recommended that Management instruct the Local Finance Committee to process the immediate return of the unutilized PDAF funds of P7,848,670.42 to the National Treasury, through the Department of Finance, in compliance with the Supreme Court Decision.  We further recommended that Management submit a written explanation why the said funds have not been returned despite the SC ruling declaring it unconstitutional.

We recommended that the City Mayor:
Require the City Engineering and City Planning and Development Offices to submit a written explanation why no immediate steps were undertaken to address the deficiencies noted for each project and for the seeming lack of initiative to enforce the measures available to correct such defects;
Implement measures and undertake catch-up programs to accelerate work accomplishments so that any delays may be promptly corrected and other courses of action/remedies may be implemented in order to recover/restore the serviceability of the damaged projects; and
Prepare an evaluation report and inventory of damaged properties/projects, subject to due validation, so that the Accounting Office can properly record the impairment thereof in the books.

We recommended that the City exhaust all means to implement without further delay through the projects outlined under the 70% Mitigation Fund of the LDRRMF. It is also recommended that the unexpended balances of prior years’ LDRRMF held in special trust be utilized upon due compliance of Section 6 NDRRMC-DBM-DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and ensure that the reprogrammed funds have specific projects and activities properly identified for effective and efficient implementation thereof towards disaster risk reduction.

We recommended that:
the LDRRM Council pass a resolution reverting the CYs 2012 to 2016 LDRRMF unspent balances to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, for adoption by the Sangguniang Panlungsod thru the City Development Council and to increase monitoring of fund utilization so that these could be used for its intended purpose before the 5-year prescription period expires;
the members of the Local Finance Committee prepare the necessary transfer of funds and accounting reports to facilitate the said reversion and certify to the availability of said funds for subsequent enactment by the local sanggunian for social services; and
the City Accountant and the LDRRM Officer reconcile the subsidiary and general ledgers for the CYs 2012 to 2016 balances to ensure the correctness of the amount to be transferred.

We recommended that the City Accountant prepare the necessary adjustments/and or corollary entries to take up the appropriate expense and revenue accounts, including Other Deferred Credits for ongoing capital outlays, so that the balances of these accounts may be fairly presented in the financial statements.  We further recommended that Management determine whether the purpose for the trust receipt was already fulfilled or the project completed so that the corresponding revenue may be taken out from the Other Deferred Credits account and the assets transferred to the General Fund.  Henceforth, ensure strict compliance to the PPSAS and the GAM for LGUs.

observations.recommendation:
Two out of the six recommendations were fully implemented in 2020.  The unimplemented recommendation are as follows:

a)	Submit the following:

DepEd-approved School Improvement Plan (SIP) and the Division Education Development Plan (DEDP) to support the necessity of this procurement; and

iii) Inventory list of lots owned by the City Government.

b)	Require the City Appraisal Committee to explain how it came up with the recommended lot price considering that the market value indicated in the new tax declaration was lower by 1/3 of the purchase cost and why it failed to come up with a negotiated price in accordance with the standards laid out in Section 12 of the IRR of RA No. 10752 in determining the market value of the lot purchased nor to exercise Section 5 of the said law;

c)	Notwithstanding the foregoing, clarify the real intent and purpose for the said lot acquisition.
observations.managementAction:
Submitted the following documents:

The Bais City Schools Division Superintendent explained that the SIP is not available since it shall commence only when the proposed Bais City School for Sports (BCSS), which he asserts is the intent of the lot purchase, becomes operational.  He likewise furnished the CY 2021 Enhanced SIP of BCNHS which includes the ongoing sports activities as initial steps towards full implementation of the BCSS, specifically under LOI No. 5, SIP No. 44/45, 


DEDP 2018-2022, with Strategy 1.1.1. - School Establishment Program as its mode of intervention and Strategy 9.1 – Strengthen sports development in the elementary, junior and senior high schools.


Inventory list of lots owned by the City Government

City Appraisal Committee  certification on the fairness of the price of the lot

Clarification that the real intent and purpose of the lot acquisition is for a school site
observations.reason:
SIP No. 44/45, LOI 5 of the Annual Implementation Plan in the ESIP of BCNHS only indicates sports development programs, the expected output of which is increased school’s performance in sports competitions and achieved through the procurement of sports equipment and holding of sports intramurals.  There was no mention of the construction of any BCSS.

It may have passed the audit team’s attention but a quick perusal of the DEDP showed that the baseline and target for Strategy 1.1 is focused mored on increasing enrollments and completers, while the key results of  Strategy 9.1 is enhanced rankings in sports competitions.  There was no mention of the need to expand the school site or create a new school in the development plan submitted.

The inventory list showed various open spaces owned by the City.  No additional comment was provided, however, why these were not considered prior to the subject acquisition.

Considering the foregoing, the actual use of the lot, regardless of the clarifications made, shall determine the eligibility of charging the lot acquisition to the SEF.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management enjoin the concerned department
heads to strictly comply with the accounting and reporting guidelines on local
roads asset management system, and to earnestly perform their respective duties
and responsibilities set forth under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated October
23, 2015.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Inventory Committee conduct the
annual physical count of all its Local Road Networks in accordance with
regulations, making sure to produce the details necessary to be reported
separately in the RPCLRN for submission to the Auditor and Accounting Office
not later than January 31 of each year. The RPCLRN shall be reconciled with
the accounting records and the necessary adjusting entries be prepared before
the statement date.

We recommended that the City Accountant prepare the necessary
adjustments/and or corollary entries to take up the appropriate expense and
revenue accounts, including Other Deferred Credits for ongoing capital outlays,
so that the balances of these accounts may be fairly presented in the financial
statements. We further recommended that Management determine whether
the purpose for the trust receipt was already fulfilled or the project completed so
that the corresponding revenue may be taken out from the Other Deferred
Credits account and the assets transferred to the General Fund. Henceforth,
ensure strict compliance to the PPSAS and the GAM for LGUs.

We reiterated our recommendations that Management require:
a) The Inventory Committee and the CGSO to prioritize the reconciliation of
the RPCPPE with the accounting records and pinpoint persons responsible
for the missing properties who should be made to pay the value thereof as
shown in the accounting records;
b) The CGSO to coordinate with the Accounting Office to fully account for
the CY 2018, 2019 and 2020 PPE purchases, complete with data on the
property tag, ARE, accountable officer, and office/location of the
equipment, otherwise, those that cannot be accounted for shall be
accordingly disallowed; and
c) The Accounting Office to prepare promptly the journal entries to take up the
SSMI, IIRUP and other asset disposals submitted by the CGSO with
complete documentation that would warrant the dropping of the same
from the books.

We recommended that the City Treasurer invariably prepare the
Schedule of Unreleased Checks on the last working day of the year and to
submit this to the City Accountant as basis in preparing the journal entry to
restore the cash in bank balance and to recognize the related liability accounts at
the end of the year, and likewise as basis for the reversing entry to be made on
the first day of the ensuing year.

We recommended that, henceforth, Management ensure that efficient
and systematic procedures are in place while strictly adhering to existing
guidelines set forth under DSWD Memorandum Circular No. 09 series of 2020
in the implementation of SAP and similar future undertakings of the
government. As for the subject transaction, we recommended that
Management comply with the documentary requirements pursuant to the audit
suspensions that may be issued.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) comply
with the mandatory posting of the updated APP, NOA, and other information
relative to the Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act in the GPPB
Online Portal as required in GPPB Circular No. 1-2020. Henceforth, we
recommended that the BAC should always be updated with the issuances and
advisories of the GPPB by visiting their website regularly to avoid any more
non-compliance in the future.

We recommended that both the City Accountant and the City Budget
Officer assign personnel to focus on the initial creation of the BGCM SAGF in
compliance with DBM LBC No. 125 and the GAM, and to henceforth
incorporate the same as a regular component of the GF books.

We recommended that Management substantiate: 1) the propriety of the
negotiated procurement and 2) the legal, technical capacity of the procurement
of the food items totaling ₱55,353,426.94 from only one supplier, providing
written explanations/justifications and documentary evidence to each of the
issues discussed above, for further evaluation.

We recommended that Management submit without further delay the
Relief Distribution Sheets which should contain the name and signature of
recipients as well as the date of receipt, and such other necessary information,
including the kind and quantity of items received to establish accountability for
the food items and relief assistance distributed.

We recommended that the DRMMO promptly prepare and submit the
utilization report supported by the distribution list which should contain the name
and signature of recipients as well as the date of receipt, and such other
necessary information, including the quantity of items received, as basis for
recognizing the corresponding expense in the books in the appropriate fiscal
period.

We recommended that the City Mayor, the City Accountant and the City
Social Welfare Development Officer send demand letters to the remaining 141
ineligible beneficiaries for the immediate refund of the illegally received subsidy
totaling ₱846,000.00 as a matter of personal recourse. It is emphasized that this
is without prejudice to the disallowance of the same amount and for refund by
the authorizing City Officials as being the ones primarily responsible for the
refund of the improperly provided subsidy prescribed under Section VIII-B.6.1
of DSWD MC No. 09, series of 2020.

We recommended that the CSWDO further validate the ineligibility of
the 113 Job Order workers and that the HRMO review the remaining 897 names
of other Job Order workers who may have been paid but were not included in
the validated list. We further recommended that the 113 Job Order workers be
required to refund the ESP given totaling ₱678,000.00, otherwise the responsible
City Officials should refund the said amount, subject to any adjustments as a
result of the CSWDO and HRMO validation of ineligible paid beneficiaries.

We recommended that the City Engineer invariably verify, before approving the Program of Work, that the City government possesses sufficient proprietary rights over the project sites for the proposed horizontal or vertical projects duly supported with the appropriate documents, including that of barangay-funded projects, and ensure that these documents (for projects funded by the City) are forwarded to the City General Services Officer who shall maintain a central file thereof, and thus shield the City from future ownership claims by third parties as well as avert losses arising therefrom. We further recommended that Management process the titling of lots donated to the City, and that the City Accountant ensure that such proof is substantially complied with by the Engineering Office as a requirement in certifying the completeness of supporting documents to disbursements for said projects.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor direct: (a) The City Engineer to – i) conduct an in-depth assessment of the accumulated work backlog of existing projects and to impose the penalties and sanctions provided under Annexes “E” and “I” of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 for those undertaken by contracts or to prioritize the completion of unfinished projects undertaken by administration; ii) improve the execution and monitoring of projects to exact compliance to project timelines by adopting stricter policies to eliminate any negative slippage noted and a detailed action to commit contractors to accomplish specific physical targets over a defined time period; iii) assign a project engineer who shall be responsible for the close supervision and reporting of the project to which he is assigned, so that proper accountability could be easily determined, as warranted; (b) The Project Monitoring Committee to – i) determine accountability for the losses sustained by the government due to projects undertaken by administration which could not anymore be put to viable use with the passage of time; ii) improve the quality of information in its monitoring reports by eliminating projects which were already reported as completed in the prior years and maintaining an updated database that highlights those which were completed during the year, still on-going and/or behind schedule; and iii) attach to the quarterly report a certification of extensions/ suspensions granted, if any, supported by a certified copy thereof.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2019
a. The City Engineer referred the matter with the City Legal Office in 2019 on how to address the issues on contractors. b. The City Legal Office sent notification letters to different contractors in the 3rd quarter of CY 2020 requesting them to submit contract suspension/resumption/extension documents and to submit written explanation on contract completion delay. c. The City Engineer requested thru the City Legal Office for the submission of the crash program of these projects. d. Fulltime Project Engineers have already been assigned to undertake close supervision of all projects undertaken by administration. e. Project monitoring committee has been created by the Chief Executive to conduct regular project monitoring and submit quarterly monitoring reports.
observations.reason: No replies received from contractors. The submission of the Quarterly Report on Programs, Projects and Activities is still very much pending; no proof of actual rectification of project defects submitted to the office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor: a) Direct the City Engineer, City Planning and Development Coordinator and/or other officials involved to submit a written explanation for the deficiencies noted, why projects undertaken by administration were not immediately corrected, with proof that no funds were released for the unfinished scope of work, or why contractors were allowed to claim progress and final payments despite the negative slippage and non-delivery of the project specifications; b) Exercise the remedies provided under Section 8.4, Annex “E” of the RIRR in addressing the contracts under default, impose and collect liquidated damages in accordance with Section 8.5 thereof and avail of the remedies under Annex “I” of the RIRR for projects that have not delivered the project requirements within the contract period; c) Justify the additional indirect cost incurred for projects undertaken by contract and why the same should not be regarded as unnecessary expenses which are not allowed under COA Circular No. 2012-003; and d) Implement measures and undertake catch-up programs to accelerate work accomplishments so that any delays may be promptly corrected and other courses of action/remedies may be availed of.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2019
a. 6 out of 11 projects were completely rectified and accordingly declared completed. b. The City Legal Office sent a notification on July 21, 2020 requesting the contractor to submit written explanation concerning contract completion targets. c. On August 12, 2020, the City Engineer requested the City Legal Office to advise the contractor to submit catch-up program. d. Fulltime Project Engineers have been assigned to undertake close supervision of all projects. e. Project monitoring committee conducts regular monitoring.
observations.reason: The City Engineer, City Planning and Development Coordinator and/or other officials involved failed to submit a written explanation for the deficiencies noted, why projects undertaken by administration were not immediately corrected, with proof that no funds were released for the unfinished scope of work, or why contractors were allowed to claim progress and final payments despite the negative slippage and non-delivery of the project specifications. No explanation/justification as to indirect costs.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation that Management give priority to the hiring of qualified personnel to fill up the mandatory DRRM positions in compliance with NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1. We likewise recommended that the City desist from charging expenditures that cannot be exclusively used for DRRM and that the allocations be realigned to other projects and activities that meet the objectives for the LDRRMF, with the lumped allocation officially revised for appropriation to a specific project that the City intends and needs to undertake in line with its disaster risk reduction priorities. Lastly, we recommended that the City through the CDRRM Office exhaust all means to implement without further delay the projects outlined under the 70% MF of the CDRRMF in order to increase their constituents’ resilience and decrease their vulnerabilities to disasters and calamities.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2019
Charges to the CDRRMF in the total amount of ₱4,076,876.00 that were earmarked under the MOOE and the Capital Outlay (CO) as follows: a. Travelling expenses – intended for emergency operation outside area jurisdiction. b. Gasoline, oil and lubricant expenses – intended for LDRRM emergency operation using DRRM vehicles. c. Other MOOE and contingency fund – intended for DRRM program activities. d. Purchase of transportation equipment – intended for ambulance, transportation vehicles for rapid disaster assessment and needs analysis operation. The LDRRM I and LDRRM II positions were filled up last October 9, 2020 and October 27, 2020, respectively.
observations.reason: The City cannot be primarily responsible for emergencies outside its jurisdiction. Fund cannot be obligated under lump-sum appropriations. The audit observation as to rate of utilization is reiterated in Part II hereof.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that on or before December 31 of each year, the City Treasurer shall send by mail individual tax bills or statements of account invariably to all taxpayers, post in all barangay halls and public places or announce by radio notices of periods during which RPT and SET may be paid to be paid without penalty, the rate of discounts for advance payment, and installment payment options. Should these strategies still fail, we further recommended and Management agreed to apply the remedies to ensure collection of the taxes due to the government by administrative action through levy on real property or by judicial action, firmly exercising the necessary procedures as mandated under RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2018
Implementation is still ongoing due to the need for permanent Revenue Collectors to handle collection activities at their official stations on weekdays thus field collection activities can be conducted only on weekends. The CTO lacks the appropriate vehicle which can transport the collectors to hinterland barangays where most of the taxpayers are residing. Activities were suspended from March 2020 to December 2020 due to the strike of COVID-19 Pandemic prohibiting face to face contact with people in the hinterlands.
observations.reason: Implementation suspended March to December 2020 due to COVID-19 pandemic; lack of vehicle and permanent revenue collectors.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that: a) The City Engineer who certified the accomplishment of the declogging works and the Assistant City Engineer who certified the payroll submit a quantification of the work done, justify the seemingly excessive man-days and corresponding payments made, and to substantiate declogging works with pictures taken during actual project implementation; b) The City Engineering Office, henceforth, prepare detailed Programs of Work with the exact location and quantified output indicated and with realistic cost estimates that would maximize the funds allocated for a specific project, complete with the plans and drawings as warranted; c) The City Development Council find more meaningful and useful projects on which to spend the bulk of its funds, which should mainly be capital in nature, in order to avoid wasteful use thereof as well as speed up the development of the City, in compliance with DILG- DBM JMC No. 2017-1; and d) The City Accountant prepare a journal entry to adjust the asset and expense accounts for the actual declogging expenses incurred.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2018
The City Engineering Office complied with and adhered to the recommendation to prepare detailed POWs as stated. Routinary declogging of canals were no longer funded in the 20% DF, thus, drainage projects only included construction and improvement activities.
observations.reason: The submission of the detailed POWs was done; however, some supporting documentation on labor and measurements remain outstanding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that: a) The LDRRM Council pass a resolution reverting the CYs 2012 and 2013 LDRRMF unspent balances to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, for adoption by the Sangguniang Panlungsod thru the City Development Council and to increase monitoring of fund utilization so that these funds could be used for its intended purpose before the 5-year prescription period expires; b) The members of the Local Finance Committee prepare the necessary transfer of funds and accounting reports to facilitate the said reversion and certify to the availability of said funds for subsequent re-appropriation for social services; and c) The City Accountant reconcile the subsidiary and general ledgers for the CY 2012 balances to ensure the correctness of the amount to be transferred.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2018
The LDRRM Council passed resolution No. 04 series of 2020 recommending the reversion of unspent balances for CY 2012, 2013 and 2014 Special Trust Fund in the amount of ₱7,325,888.85 to unappropriated surplus of the General Fund for Social Services.
observations.reason: As presented in the Report on the Utilization of LDRRMF as of December 31, 2020, the 2012, 2013 and 2014 unutilized balances were still under the Special Trust Fund Books, hence the balances were not yet reverted back to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to direct the GFPS, DILG and CPDO to establish the GAD database as storage of necessary information in aid of effective gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-1 to ensure that the PPAs are aligned with GAD-related undertakings so that the intended beneficiaries are assured of the services, protection, and other assistance that may be provided. We further recommended and Management agreed to exclude from the PPAs projects not eligible for charging to the fund and for the GFPS to prioritize closer monitoring of project implementation.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2018
a. Conducted GAD Focal Point System Coordination Conference on GAD Database Development on July 16, 2020. The establishment of GAD database is still on-going. b. Sent communication to all offices on the submission of Sex Disaggregated Data on July 21, 2020. c. Consolidated Sex Disaggregated Data.
observations.reason: Establishment of GAD database is still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LPRAT, through the BUB Focal Person, intensify efforts to fast-track the implementation of the BUB program by assigning technical personnel who will focus on BUB projects and by closely monitoring progress thereon for the LGU to achieve its target of reducing poverty and likewise avert material repercussions that may result from its low implementation rate. We further recommended that the City Accountant review prior year transactions and make the necessary adjusting entries in order to reconcile the subsidiary ledgers with the actual fund utilization.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2017
Completed the CY 2015 BUB project for road construction worth ₱1.5 million. The CY 2016 BUB project for road concreting worth ₱2.0 million did not materialize due to site issue. The BAC Office advised to return the project funds.
observations.reason: No comment was provided as to the remaining projects, including any comment from the City Accountant as to the necessary adjustments.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the BAC and TWG be properly supervised and be made accountable for lapses in the performance of their functions, as warranted. The BAC should indispensably prepare the checklists and require conformity in fulfilling all the official requirements indicated therein in order to preserve the integrity of the bidding process as mandated under RA No. 9184. We further recommended that Management comply with the requirements/documents contained in the Notice of Suspension to be issued to enable us to make a decision in audit.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2017
1. A “No second-hand” policy was adopted in 2018 to present, hence, there has been no procurement of second-hand/reconditioned vehicle/equipment since 2018 to present. 2. Likewise adopted policies in case of acquisition of second-hand/reconditioned vehicle/equipment will be inevitable. 3. All vehicles/equipment procured since 2018 to 2020 were brand new.
observations.reason: Awaiting issuance of Notice of Suspension. The BAC was advised to comply with the requirements in the Notice of Suspension; no further procurement of second-hand equipment was made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the seller, Don Gaspar Vicente, Inc., to refund the amount of P7,833,429.81 representing the cost of the road lot and the Environmental Protection lot, both of which may not be the subject of sale pursuant to Presidential Decree No. 1216 otherwise, the loss shall be borne by officials and personnel determined to be responsible for the transaction which is grossly disadvantageous to the government.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2016
On April 4, 2017, stock certificates of BACONSEPZ owned by the Local Government of Bais were properly turned-over for recovery of investments. On July 13, 2018, Atty. Ignacio L Vicente/BACONSEPZ paid to the City Treasurer the amount of ₱330,000.00 with OR No. 0969639.
observations.reason: Management has not taken full action on the remaining balance of ₱600,000.00 which has yet to be recovered from BACONSEPZ.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, as representative of the City of Bais, request the treasurer of the League of Cities of the Philippines to acknowledge its payments with government Accountable Form No. 51 in order that the funds may be subjected to government accounting and auditing rules and regulations.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2016
Made formal letter and follow-up calls to LCP. Received an electronic copy of the Accountable Form No. 51 sent through email last March 10, 2021.
observations.reason: Only an electronic copy of AF No. 51 is in the hands of the city government. Still awaiting the original copy to be sent through LBC by LCP.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor file a request to the Commission on Audit for the write-off of dormant accounts receivable following the procedures outlined under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 in order to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements. In addition, we recommended that the city exert effort in the collection of receivables aged two to 10 years amounting to ₱1,817,468.88 to avoid loss of government funds.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2016
Request letter from the City Mayor’s Office was forwarded to Commission on Audit on January 3, 2020. Prepared quarterly Schedule of Dormant Receivable Accounts (Annexes 1, 1A, 1B, 1C).
observations.reason: Request is still subject to evaluation and covers only ₱137,782.41, excluding the ₱1.8 million receivables.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the immediate disposal by public auction or sale of the Honda CRF250 motorcycles in accordance with Budget Circular No. 2010-2 and henceforth, strict compliance with existing regulations on the purchase of motor vehicles. We further recommended that any price difference between the cost and the auction sale proceeds be made a personal liability of the approving officers so that the full amount shall be restituted to the government.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2015
The Disposal Committee already conducted auction sale on December 17, 2020 based on the Net Book Value/Salvage Value (₱99,349.65) determined by the City Accountant with one (1) unit sold at an auction price of ₱105,000 OR#1194232 and OR#1206295. The other unit was scheduled for re-auction together with other items.
observations.reason: One unit is still subject for disposal.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Agriculturist supervise closely the Dispersal Program and see to it that proper records are kept and that all movement (birth of offspring, death, transfers) are completely recorded in their records and reported to the GSO and Accounting Offices. In case of death or disposal, the program manager be required to facilitate the request for relief of accountability, or to require payment of the value of the animal if warranted under the circumstances.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2013
Conducting proper records and contacts related to Annual Dispersal Program. Back tracing of recipients, original stocks and offspring currently being closely conducted by the department/personnel involved during the Target Implementation Date (time frame), with regular submission thereof.
observations.reason: The City Agriculturist is still in the process of close supervision in the preparation of reports and regular submission thereof.

observations.recommendation: We reiterate our prior year’s recommendation that management stop the practice of hiring employees on job order basis and instead issue appointments in plantilla form to be recorded to the appropriate accounts under Personal Services. Henceforth, always observe the required Personal Services limitation.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2012
The City placed presently engaged J.O. personnel on rotation basis. Minimized the hiring of additional J.O. personnel. On March 4, 2021, the HRMO IV requested the SP Members through the City Mayor to create 40 positions of Adm. Aide III (Driver I) in the CEO as an initial step to address this concern. Management acted on the recommendation favorably and initially created 48 positions of Adm. Aide I (Utility Worker I) positions and issued appointment in plantilla forms since 2017. As of December 31, 2020, there were 2,259 JOs hired by the City as reported by the HRMO, a decrease of 1,241 from CY 2012, but still an increase of 472 from CY 2019.
observations.reason: There was reduction in number of JOs compared to earlier years but still substantial; further actions ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require: 
a) The Inventory Committee and the CGSO to prioritize the reconciliation of the RPCPPE with the accounting records, update the information required in the schedule of additions and disposals of PPE, and pinpoint persons responsible for the missing properties; b) The CGSO to coordinate with the Accounting Office to fully account for the CY 2018 and 2019 PPE purchases, otherwise, those that cannot be accounted for shall be accordingly disallowed; and c) The Accounting Office to prepare promptly the journal entries to take up the Summaries of Supplies and Materials Issued after a thorough evaluation of its completeness, and for the same office to coordinate with the GSO in order to address long-existing inventories which have not been accounted for and to determine accountability therefor.

We recommended that the City make a written follow up with the COA-ICFAU for the credit advice on the liquidation reports already submitted and to validate submission of the LRs for the cash advances granted from CYs 2011-2013. Should there be confirmation of the non-submission of the LRs for the latter, we recommended that the City demand from the former City Mayor the immediate settlement/liquidation thereof. Henceforth, comply strictly with the documentary and other requirements for C/IF expenses prescribed in the JC and likewise with the Omnibus Election Code for similar occurrences in the future in order to avert any offense punishable under the Code.

We recommended that the City Accountant and the City Treasurer review these recorded payables to ascertain if these still represent valid claims, otherwise seek authority from the Sangguniang Panlungsod to revert these to the Unappropriated Surplus in the General Fund so that these may be made available for funding valuable development programs and other priority activities of the City and so that the liabilities would be fairly presented in the financial statements. We further recommended that the important details which are lacking be adequately presented to aid users of financial statements in decision-making and to exhaust all means to determine the cause for the existing negative balances so that any errors could be corrected and overpayments recovered.

We recommended that the City Engineer invariably verify, before approving the Program of Work, that the City government possesses sufficient proprietary rights over the project sites for the proposed horizontal or vertical projects duly supported with the appropriate documents, including that of barangay-funded projects, and ensure that these documents (for projects funded by the City) are forwarded to the City General Services Officer who shall maintain a central file thereof, and thus shield the City from future ownership claims by third parties as well as avert losses arising therefrom. We further recommended that Management process the titling of lots donated to the City, and that the City Accountant ensure that such proof is substantially complied with by the Engineering Office as a requirement in certifying the completeness of supporting documents to disbursements for said projects.

We recommended that Management:
a) Submit the following:
i) certification by the City Treasurer and City Accountant showing the amount of SEF collections in CY 2018 and January to July 2019 which were made under protest and prove that these were not included in the funding for SEF Supplemental Budget No. 3, s. 2019;
ii) DepEd-approved School Improvement Plan (SIP) and the Division Education Development Plan (DEDP) to support the necessity of this procurement; and
iii) Inventory list of lots owned by the City Government.
b) Require the City Appraisal Committee to explain how it came up with the recommended lot price considering that the market value indicated in the new tax declaration was lower by 1/3 of the purchase cost and why it failed to come up with a negotiated price in accordance with the standards laid out in Section 12 of the IRR of RA No. 10752 in determining the market value of the lot purchased nor to exercise Section 5 of the said law;
c) Notwithstanding the foregoing, clarify the real intent and purpose for the said lot acquisition; and
d) Henceforth, ensure that an independent appraisal is generated by securing assistance from the Provincial Appraisal Committee should it not meet the requirements for coming up with its own local appraisal committee or where conflict of interest exists.

We recommended that Management establish a procedure for identifying PHWs and PSWs who shall be exposed to high risk or low risk hazard including the duration of such exposure duly approved by the Secretary of Health to support claims for hazard pay. We further recommended that the City Accountant require all PHWs and PSWs to support their claims for hazard pay with proof of actual exposure to specific health hazards and occupational risks with certifications from appropriate agencies and the documents required under Section 5.8 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 as an indispensable requirement.

We recommended that Management: (a) establish clear-cut guidelines for the grant of communication allowances, taking into consideration the economy and equity in determining who shall be entitled to such in relation to actual communication needs of the City, and (b) cease the implementation of the increase in cellcard allowance being excessive as contemplated under COA Circular No. 2012-003.

We recommended and Management agreed that on or before December 31 of each year, the City Treasurer shall send mail individual tax bills or statements of account invariably to all taxpayers, post in all barangay halls and public places or announce by radio notices of periods during which RPT and SET may be paid without penalty, the rate of discounts for advance payment, and installment payment options. Should these strategies still fail, we further recommended and Management agreed to apply the remedies to ensure collection of the taxes due to the government by administrative action through levy on real property or by judicial action, firmly exercising the necessary procedures as mandated under RA No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: AAR conducted massive tax collection last December 31 of 2018 and tax information campaign at various barangays and coordinated with its Barangay Officials and Collectors to handle collection activities at their official stations on weekdays. This office also lacks the appropriate vehicle which can transport the collectors to hinterland barangays where most of the taxpayers are residing.
observations.reason: Implementation is still ongoing due to the need for permanent Revenue collectors to handle collection activities at their official stations on weekdays thus field collection activities can be conducted only on weekdays. This office also lacks the appropriate vehicle which can transport the collectors to hinterland barangays where most of the taxpayers are residing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management thru the City Accountant agreed to: a) Send demand letters for the immediate liquidation of unliquidated cash advances, withhold payment of any money owing to the AO after due notice, and henceforth, strictly observe the requirements/limitations stipulated under Section 89 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 96-004; and b) Maintain updated index cards for cash advances and submit the quarterly report with all required information on or before the 10th day following the end of the quarter..
observations.managementAction: Demand letters were sent to last July 3, 2019 and October 16, 2019. The balance was reduced by P407,219.00. Index cards have already been maintained and updated.
observations.reason: Note: Implemented as recommendation but a substantial amount of unliquidated cash advances totaling P37,409,444.55 still exist at year-end 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Accountant prepare a journal entry to adjust the asset and expense accounts for the actual declogging expenses incurred. We further recommended that: a) The City Engineer who certified the accomplishment of the declogging works and the Assistant City Engineer who certified the payroll submit a quantification of the work done, justify the seemingly excessive man-days and corresponding payments made, and to substantiate declogging works with pictures taken during actual project implementation; b) The City Engineering Office, henceforth, prepare detailed Programs of Work with the exact location and quantified output indicated and with realistic cost estimates that would maximize the funds allocated for a specific project, complete with the plans and drawings as warranted; c) Management consider charging routinary declogging expenses to the General Fund Proper based on a valid POW and to refrain from appropriating the 20% DF for non-capital or non-investment expenditures; d) The City Development Council find more meaningful and useful projects on which to spend the bulk of its funds, which should mainly be capital in nature, in order to avoid wasteful use thereof as well as speed up the development of the City, in compliance with DILG- DBM JMC No. 2017-1; and e) The City Accountant prepare a journal entry to adjust the asset and expense accounts for the actual declogging expenses incurred.
observations.managementAction: CY 2019 20% EDF Projects with declogging works were subjected to revision of POWs by the CEO; while corresponding job orders issued & payrolls prepared indicated the location and volume of clearing works as component of construction of drainage canals/system. In the CY 2020 AIP, no funds were allotted under the 20% EDF for declogging of canals. JEV nos. 300-18-06-68 were prepared to adjust the books of the Trust Fund and corresponding JEV nos. 100-2018-06-256 to 100-2018-06-258 & JEV no. 100-2018-06-281 were also prepared to adjust the books of the General Fund per COA recommendations.
observations.reason: No comment given by Management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a) Instruct the Division Chief to stop further spending of the government funds for distribution lines and facilities of NORECO I and to submit the MOA for further evaluation in audit; b) Submitted request for realignment/reversion of the existing CY 2019 Lateral Line projects in the amount of Php2.4M to the CMO (Funds were reverted per SP Res. No. 558 Ord. No. 18 dated December 12, 2019.); and c) No more appropriation was recommended for the lateral line projects for CY 2020.
observations.managementAction: Instructed the Division Chief to stop further spending of the mentioned projects. Last payroll was in October 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed that: a) The LDRRM Council pass a resolution reverting the CYs 2012 and 2013 LDRRMF unspent balances to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund, for adoption by the Sangguniang Panlungsod thru the City Development Council and to increase monitoring of fund utilization so that these could be used for its intended purpose before the 5-year prescription period expires; b) The members of the Local Finance Committee prepare the necessary transfer of funds and accounting reports to facilitate the said reversion and certify to the availability of said funds for subsequent re-appropriation for social services; and c) The City Accountant reconcile the subsidiary and general ledgers for the CY 2012 balances to ensure the correctness of the amount to be transferred.
observations.managementAction: On-going reconciliation.
observations.reason: Transfer of the balances was not implemented. Partially Implemented assigned person to CTO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management give priority to the hiring of qualified personnel to fill up the mandatory DRRM positions in compliance with NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1. We likewise recommended that the City desist from charging expenditures that cannot be exclusively used for DRRM and that the ineligible allocations be realigned to other projects and activities that meet the objectives for the LDRRMF, with the lumped allocation officially revised for appropriation to a specific project that the City intends and needs to undertake in line with its disaster risk reduction priorities. Lastly, we recommended that the City through the CDRRM Office exhaust all means to implement without further delay the projects outlined under the 70% MF of the CDRRMF in order to increase their constituents’ resilience and decrease their vulnerabilities to disasters and calamities.
observations.managementAction: Acted on this recommendation favourably and initially created 48 positions of Administrative Aide I (Utility Worker I) and issued 84 appointment in plantilla forms last 2017 & 2018 but unfortunately the Sangguniang Panlalawigan of Negros Oriental invalidated the said creation. The letter request to the Local Chief Executive for the filling up of the mandatory vacant positions has not been prepared, hence not acted upon.
observations.reason: The CDRRMO is still understaffed; the mandated positions have not been fully filled due to the invalidation by the provincial sanggunian and no further action on filling up positions.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to direct the GFPS, DILG and CPDO to establish the GAD database as storage of necessary information in aid of effective gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation in accordance with PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-1 and to exclude from the PPAs projects not eligible for charging to the fund and for the GFPS to prioritize closer monitoring of project implementation.
observations.managementAction: Conducted Sex Disaggregated Data Profiling Orientation Workshop on May 15-17, 2019; GFPS meeting on July 8, 2019 and consultations with the BAC on November 5 & 11, 2019. Closer review of the City’s GAD Plan and Budget. Included the GAD PPAs in the Monitoring Program of the City’s Project Monitoring Committee.
observations.reason: Database not yet completely formulated since data-gathering is still on-going.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to fully provide the 5% allocation for GAD based on the total annual appropriations, including CEEs, in accordance with Section 36(a) of RA No. 9710 so that the envisioned goals of the program, such as gender-responsive governance, protection of women’s human rights, and the promotion of women’s economic empowerment, can be achieved through the implementation of sufficiently-funded PPAs.
observations.managementAction: Rectified this error and ensured that a proper allocation was made in the succeeding years.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) The CDRRMC review the unutilized balances of the LDRRMF as of December 31, 2017 and revise the annual LDRRMFIP in accordance with the format prescribed under COA Circular No. 2012-002 which would include both current and prior year balances, strictly observing the 5-year validity period; b) The CDRRMC ensure that the LDRRMFIP is duly incorporated in the Local Development Plan and Annual Work and Financial Plan duly approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod and the Local Chief Executive, including supplemental budgets/realignments, otherwise the repercussions of Section 87 of PD No. 1445 shall apply accordingly; c) The City Budget Officer present the LDRRMF in the SAAOB in accordance with the breakdown and codes specified under Section 5.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; and d) Management submit the corresponding CDRRMC and SP Resolutions and revised LDRRMFIP and AIPs authorizing the utilization of P4.28 million from the CYs 2012 and 2013 balances transferred to the TF.
observations.managementAction: The CDRRMC convened and conducted a review on the utilization of the LDRRMF. The council approved the 2019 Annual Investment Plan and reflected the unutilized balances of the LDRRMF of the previous years and it was duly approved by the Sangguniang Panlungsod.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management ensure that the LDRRMF is fairly presented in the financial statements by strictly complying with the accounting and reporting policies outlined in COA Circular No. 2012-002 and by undertaking the following: a) The Budget Officer should maintain separate RAAOs for the MF-MOOE and MF-CO, and revise the SAAOB to include the P8,983,513.00 as part of the Continuing Appropriations under the LDRRMF; and b) The Accountant should prepare the proposed adjusting entries as detailed in Annex B hereof, and in addition: revise the RSU to incorporate both the CY and PY balances with corresponding utilizations; adequately disclose sufficient and relevant information on the LDRRMF in the Notes to FS in accordance with Section 5.1.16 of the Circular and the related prescribed format; and coordinate with the Budget Officer and review prior year balances for possible similar errors.
observations.managementAction: JEV nos. 300-18-06-68 were prepared to adjust the books of the Trust Fund and corresponding JEV nos. 100-2018-06-256 to 100-2018-06-258 & JEV no. 100-2018-06-281 were also prepared to adjust the books of the General Fund per COA recommendations. Disclosed sufficient & relevant info on the LDRRM on the Notes to FS for CY 2019 per COA recommendation. Coordinated with the Budget Officer and review prior year balances for possible similar errors.

observations.recommendation:
RECOMMENDATIONS:
We recommended that:
a) The Inventory Committee and the City General Services Office (CGSO)
reconcile the results thereof with the accounting records, prepare the report for
submission to the Auditor and pinpoint responsibility for missing properties;
b) The CGSO secure all copies of the ARE and update the filing thereof,
ensuring that these are principally considered especially during the separation of
accountable officers from the government service;
c) Management update the schedule of PPE additions and disposals for CY
2017, complete with data on the property tag, ARE, accountable officer, office/location
of the equipment, otherwise, those that cannot be accounted for shall be accordingly
disallowed; and
d) The City Accountant attach the formatted and detailed schedule of PPE
additions and disposals to the yearly financial report of the LGU and regularly update
it to facilitate the conduct of the physical inventory and the determination of
accountability for government properties.

RECOMMENDATIONS:
We recommended that Management exhaust all means to implement without
further delay the projects funded out of the 20% DF and fast track the backlogs in
prior years in order to minimize idle funds and maximize the usefulness of such
projects. We further recommended that the functions of the City Planning and
Development Office (CPDO) and the Engineering Office be clearly delineated and
coordination mechanisms be in place to facilitate the planning, execution, monitoring
and reporting of project implementation. Any favorable action given hereto will
ultimately redound to the benefit of the City and its constituents, and to the
achievement of the overall objective of R.A. 7160 to make LGUs self-reliant
communities.

RECOMMENDATION:
We recommended that the LPRAT, through the BUB Focal Person, intensify
efforts to fast-track the implementation of the BUB program by assigning technical
personnel who will focus on BUB projects and by closely monitoring progress thereon
for the LGU to achieve its target of reducing poverty and likewise avert any
repercussions that may result from its low implementation rate. We further
recommended that the City Accountant review prior year transactions and make the
necessary adjusting entries in order to reconcile the subsidiary ledgers with the actual
fund utilization.

RECOMMENDATION:
We recommended that the City Accountant recognize the appropriate
depreciation expense for the subject PPE acquisitions using the Prior Years’
Adjustment and Accumulated Depreciation accounts. We further recommended that
the PPE Schedule be updated to include the date of acquisition of the asset. We
likewise recommended that financial reports be properly reviewed for the timely
detection of errors and preparation of the necessary adjustments to ensure that
accounts are fairly presented in the financial statements.

RECOMMENDATION:
We recommended that the Mayor direct the Treasurer and the Accountant to
account for the differences noted between the ledger and cashbook balances, and
henceforth conduct a quarterly reconciliation of their individual records or as often as
necessary.

RECOMMENDATIONS:
We recommended that the BAC and Technical Working Group (TWG) be
properly supervised and be made accountable for lapses in the performance of their
functions, as warranted. The BAC should indispensably prepare the checklists and
require conformity in fulfilling all the official requirements indicated therein in order
to preserve the integrity of the bidding process as mandated under R.A. 9184.
We further recommended that Management comply with the
requirements/documents contained in the Notice of Suspension to be issued to enable
us to make a decision in audit.

RECOMMENDATION:
We recommended that Management immediately stop the payment of the
employer’s share of Pag-IBIG Fund premiums in excess of P100.00 per employee and
require employee-beneficiaries, including all officials who authorized such excessive
contributions, to refund excess premiums to the government.

RECOMMENDATIONS:
We recommended that the budget for the LCPC be allocated in the annual
budget as a separate appropriation computed at not less than 1% of the IRA. We
further recommended that the Council ensure its functionality through regular
meetings and give priority to the formulation and implementation of PPAs aligned with
DILG MC No. 2012-120 and the City’s Children’s Welfare Code enacted in CY 2010,
and desist from utilizing the fund for payment of honoraria or financial assistance and
the like as these are not allowable charges to the fund. Lastly, we recommended that
the Council prepare the Accomplishment Report in order to provide proper monitoring
for the attainment of the program’s objectives.

RECOMMENDATIONS:
We recommended that Management:
a) Terminate the contract for default following the procedures outlined under
Annex “I” of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184, without prejudice to other courses of
action and remedies available under the circumstances;
b) File a claim with Stronghold Insurance Company, Inc. to recover the
advance payment made to YSL Builders;
c) Impose the maximum of 10% as liquidated damages on YSL Builders in
accordance with Section 8.5, Annex “I” of the 2016 Revised IRR of R.A. 9184;
d) Furnish our office a copy of the performance security posted by the
contractor, if any, and forfeit the same in favor of the city; otherwise, if there is none,
direct the members of the BAC to explain in writing the lack thereof;
e) Direct the City Engineer or City Planning and Development Coordinator
(CPDC) to submit a written explanation why no immediate steps were undertaken to
address this deficiency; and
f) Implement measures and establish coordination mechanisms to ensure that
projects are closely monitored so that any delays may be promptly corrected and other
courses of action/remedies may be availed of.

RECOMMENDATION:
We recommended that Management fast-track the completion of the updated
ISWMP so it can be approved at the earliest possible time and thus provide effective
guidance to the LGU in undertaking programs for the protection of the environment.

We recommended that in the AAR Only appropriations for the determination of the appropriations Peace and Order/Public Safety, only those appropriations which are to be utilized directly for the maintenance of peace and order and public safety as enumerated under Sec. 3.23 of Joint Circular No. 1 of COA, DILG, DBM, GCGOCCs and DND be included in the list in order to correctly compute the amount of Confidential Fund.
observations.managementAction: The Mayor issued a directive to comply with the Joint Circular. Only those within the list of allowable projects shall be charged to 20%.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the government insure with the General Insurance Fund properties covered by insurance amounting to more than P200 million in order that the city can be indemnified for damages to or losses of properties due to fire, earthquake, floods, storms or other calamities.
observations.managementAction: Of the insurable assets, P347,774,000 buildings insured, properties not yet P27,940,000 equipment insured.
observations.reason: Some assets are occupied/utilized by national offices.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that henceforth, all collections made by any employee of the city government in connection with the city fiesta be treated as public funds, to be acknowledged by official receipts or cash tickets and remitted to the city treasurer for the proper accounting thereof. Expenses shall then be covered by duly approved vouchers and subjected to the audit jurisdiction of the Commission on Audit.
observations.managementAction: No employee was allowed to solicit nor receive donations for the fiesta celebration. Ticket sales and income from stall rentals were remitted to the CTO.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that in the AAR identification of projects to be charged to the 20% Development Fund, the guidelines and limitations under DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2011-1 must be strictly observed.
observations.managementAction: The Mayor issued a directive to comply with the Joint Circular. Only those within the list of allowable projects shall be charged to 20%.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the allocation of at least one percent of the IRA for the programs of the LCPC, the creation of a CIMTF and the activation and strengthening of the LCPC through the following measures:

a. Requiring the LCPC to conduct regular and special meetings at least once every two months to discuss issues and problems on children and to come up with strategies for their implementation;

b. Requiring the LCPC to maintain an updated database of children as basis for the preparation of the AWFP for the amount allocated, which should be submitted to and approved by the local sanggunian; and

c. Specifying the responsibilities of each of the members of the LCPC and the management and referral of children in need of protection and assistance, as well as the conduct of follow-up; and

d. Requiring the LCPC to perform their mandated functions and submit the required plans as enumerated under Sec. 3 of DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2008-126 dated August 21, 2008.
observations.managementAction: 1% of IRA allocated to various programs for children with the CSWD,CHO and CCRO are also part of other committees.
observations.reason: Regular meetings were not conducted since 2016. Only one (1) LCPC meeting was conducted. No updating of database. Deficiency in allocation was discussed in a separate observation in Part II of the report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the immediate revocation of the orders exempting officials and personnel from the biometrics timekeeping system, except for the ambulance drivers who are on call 24 hours a day, the Mayor’s security personnel and drivers as they keep the same hours as the Mayor, and the JO assigned to the Slaughterhouse. We further recommended the reassignment of the eight JO watchmen from the Mayor’s and her son’s respective residences to the City Hall or other official premises, otherwise their wages may be a charge to their personal funds, while no wages may be paid to the two JO utility workers who were neither found at station nor known to the city hall personnel. Lastly, we recommended a reduction to the barest possible minimum the number of JOs hired to drive the Mayor’s two service vehicles.

We recommended that henceforth, requisitions for fuel from the storage tank should be covered by duly-approved Requisition and Issue Slips and the quantity of fuel issued or recorded to the Driver’s Trip Ticket, to be accounted for until RIS. We further recommended that the Driver’s Trip Tickets be properly and completely accomplished to show all the required information, including the names of passengers with their signatures, in order that the reasonableness of fuel consumption can be determined. We also recommended that management ensure that all vehicles are equipped with working odometers, and that the trip ticket form be corrected to conform to the prescribed form (Annex 17).
observations.managementAction: Revision of Driver’s Trip Tickets to conform with standard format prescribed by COA was undertaken. Issuance of fuel can only be done upon presentation of daily approved trip ticket and Repair of odometers undertaken. Repair of odometers was still ongoing.
observations.reason: Most drivers have difficulties in filling up the needed information in trip tickets.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Mayor, as representative of the City of Bais, request the treasurer of the League of Cities of the Philippines to acknowledge its payments (as well as collections from other member-cities) with government Accountable Form No. 51 in order that the funds may be subjected to government accounting and auditing rules and regulations.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor file a request to the Commission on Audit for the write-off of dormant accounts receivable following the procedures outlined under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 in order to fairly present the accounts in the financial statements. In addition, we recommended that the city exert effort in the collection of receivables aged two to 10 years amounting to P1,817,468.88 to avoid loss of government funds.
observations.managementAction: The City Accountant submitted the request to COA. The same was returned, however, for further requirements. Attachments or supporting documents to the letter request for write-off like death certificates are not readily available.
observations.reason: The primary reason for the existence of dormant accounts is the absence of documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the allocation and that henceforth, OTS be remunerated through CTO and that remuneration in cash be authorized only in exceptional cases when application of CTO would adversely affect the operations of the city.
